module: FA COPD and Asthma Part II

questions.question: A 65-year-old man with COPD is admitted with a 2-day history of worsening dyspnoea and purulent sputum. His last lung function test (6 months earlier) showed FEV1 45% predicted and FEV1/FVC 0.60. Based on this lung function test, in which GOLD stage of severity is this patient classified?
questions.explanation: Baseline COPD severity is graded by the post-bronchodilator GOLD spirometric classification (all stages require FEV1/FVC <0.70): Stage 1 (mild) FEV1 >=80% predicted; Stage 2 (moderate) FEV1 50-79%; Stage 3 (severe) FEV1 30-49%; Stage 4 (very severe) FEV1 <30%, or <50% with chronic respiratory failure. An FEV1 of 45% predicted falls in the Stage 3 (severe) range.

questions.question: In a patient with COPD, which of the following should the assessment of symptoms include?
questions.explanation: Symptom assessment in COPD should cover severity of breathlessness, cough, sputum production, wheezing and chest tightness, as well as weight loss/anorexia, change in alertness or mental status, fatigue, confusion, anxiety, dizziness, pallor and cyanosis - so all of the listed items are relevant.

questions.question: A 65-year-old man with an acute exacerbation of COPD has hypoxaemia (SpO2 86% on room air), fever, and bilateral wheeze on examination. Which of the following tests are you considering performing at this stage?
options: D: Chest CT with intravenous contrast
E: Measurement of Peak expiratory flow (PEF)
questions.explanation: Arterial blood gases are essential to obtain an accurate oxygenation status and identify hypercapnia in acute or acute-on-chronic respiratory failure. Spirometry is required to diagnose COPD but is not practical during an acute episode; alpha-1-antitrypsin level is relevant only to initial outpatient work-up, not acute management; contrast CT is not part of the initial assessment of an exacerbation (though it may later help identify conditions mimicking or complicating it, e.g. pulmonary embolism); PEF is useful for assessing asthma severity, but its role in acute COPD exacerbation is unproven.

questions.question: Which of the following is an indication for ICU admission in acute exacerbation of COPD?

statements.label: B
statements.text: Inadequate response to initial therapy (severe dyspnoea, altered mental status)

statements.label: C
statements.text: Persistent or worsening hypoxaemia (PaO2 <5.3 kPa / 40 mmHg) despite supplemental oxygen and bronchodilation

statements.label: D
statements.text: Persistent or worsening respiratory acidosis (pH <7.25) despite non-invasive ventilation

questions.explanation: [UNCONFIRMED] Indications for ICU admission in acute exacerbation of COPD include inadequate response to initial therapy, persistent/worsening hypoxaemia despite oxygen and bronchodilator therapy, persistent/worsening respiratory acidosis despite non-invasive ventilation, need for invasive mechanical ventilation, and haemodynamic instability requiring vasopressors. Worsening respiratory ACIDOSIS (not alkalosis) is the concerning direction, so statement A is false. The source material's headline answer key ('FTTF') marks statement D as false, which directly contradicts its own accompanying feedback text explicitly listing this exact scenario as an ICU indication - corrected to True here. The PaO2 threshold has also been corrected to 40 mmHg (matching both the feedback text and the correct mmHg equivalent of 5.3 kPa) - the question's original '60 mmHg' does not match 5.3 kPa and appears to be a transcription error in the source.

questions.question: Which of the following is associated with a high risk of severe COPD exacerbation?
options: A: A marked increase in symptoms or change in vital signs
C: At least three exacerbations in the previous 12 months
D: Severe baseline COPD (FEV1/FVC ratio less than 0.70 and FEV1 less than 50 percent of predicted)
questions.explanation: Factors associated with increased risk of severe COPD exacerbation include the severity of underlying COPD (GOLD classification), duration of worsening or new symptoms, number of previous exacerbations (at least three in the previous 12 months), comorbidities, current treatment regimen, prior history of mechanical ventilation, clinical signs of respiratory distress, paradoxical chest wall movement, new-onset central cyanosis, peripheral oedema, haemodynamic instability, and deteriorated mental status - so all of the listed factors are relevant.

questions.question: In making the triage decision as to whether a patient with an acute exacerbation of COPD or asthma needs ventilatory support initiated quickly, important signs include the following, EXCEPT:
options: A: Inadequate response to initial therapy
questions.explanation: [UNCONFIRMED] ESICM showed no facit and no feedback text for this question after an incorrect answer. Best clinical reasoning: an isolated PaCO2 threshold is not itself a validated trigger for urgent ventilatory support, since some patients (particularly chronic CO2 retainers) tolerate a chronically elevated PaCO2 - it is the accompanying pH/acidosis, altered mental status, hypoxaemia and haemodynamic instability that indicate urgency, consistent with the rest of this module. Verify against the original ESICM material if possible.

questions.question: A 32-year-old man with a severe asthma attack is admitted to the ED at 20:00 hrs. He is given steroids, inhaled beta-adrenergic drugs and inhaled anticholinergic therapy. After two hours of observation and treatment, his vital signs and arterial blood gases have not improved. On 10 litres of oxygen by open mask he now has: BP 140/70 mmHg, pulse 130/min, respiratory rate 28/min, audible wheeze, and is unable to speak in full sentences. ABG: pH 7.23, PaO2 10 kPa, PaCO2 7.9 kPa, HCO3 23 mmol/L, BE -2 mmol/L. What is your action at this time?
options: A: Transfer to an intermediate care unit for further medical treatment
B: Transfer to an ICU for further observation
C: Transfer to an ICU for immediate intubation and IPPV
D: Keep the patient in the ED for another 2-3 hours
E: Transfer to the ICU for non-invasive ventilation
questions.explanation: [UNCONFIRMED] A progressive rise in PaCO2 despite optimal treatment for severe asthma is a sign of impending respiratory exhaustion and a predictor of fatal asthma, occurring even before severe hypercapnia develops. Such a patient should be transferred to ICU for close monitoring and early identification of the need for intubation, rather than committing immediately to intubation or remaining in a lower-acuity setting. No option letter or green check was shown by ESICM - inferred from feedback text stating the patient should be 'admitted in ICU for close monitoring and early identification for intubation need'.
questions.topic: severe asthma ICU triage rising PaCO2

questions.question: Which of the following factors indicate that a COPD exacerbation should be treated in the hospital rather than at home?

statements.label: D
statements.text: Insidious or gradual onset of symptoms

questions.explanation: [UNCONFIRMED] Acute confusion is a recognised marker of severity warranting hospital treatment; mild breathlessness alone is not. No explanatory feedback text was shown by ESICM for this question - the True/False pattern above reproduces ESICM's marked answer key exactly, but the underlying rationale for C and D is not elaborated in the source (a PaO2 of 60 mmHg sits at the classic threshold used to define respiratory failure rather than clearly below it, which may explain why it is not marked as an independent indication on its own; the rationale for marking gradual/insidious onset as True is unclear from the available material).
questions.topic: hospital versus home treatment COPD exacerbation

questions.question: In a patient with an exacerbation of COPD or asthma, warning signs of impending respiratory arrest include the following, EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: [UNCONFIRMED] ESICM showed no facit and no feedback text for this question after an incorrect answer. Best clinical reasoning: severe hypoxaemia, lethargy, a silent chest, and pulsus paradoxus are all classic markers of life-threatening/near-fatal asthma or COPD. A heart rate of 110/min is only modest tachycardia (often simply reflecting beta-agonist treatment) and is not itself a marker of impending arrest - marked bradycardia or a falling heart rate would be more concerning. Verify against the original ESICM material if possible.

questions.question: Which of the following are risk factors for asthma-related death?

statements.label: A
statements.text: Food allergy in a patient with asthma

statements.label: B
statements.text: A history of psychiatric disease or psychosocial problems

statements.label: D
statements.text: Any hospitalisation or emergency care visit for asthma

questions.explanation: [UNCONFIRMED] Recognised risk factors for asthma-related death include food allergy in an asthmatic patient, psychiatric or psychosocial problems, and over-use of short-acting beta2-agonists. No explanatory feedback text was shown by ESICM for statement D; it is transcribed as False per ESICM's marking, though clinically the qualifier likely matters - hospitalisation or an ED visit for asthma specifically within the past year is the well-established risk factor, whereas 'any' such visit ever, unqualified by recency, is a broader and less validated claim, which may explain why it is marked false as worded.
